text: In May 2022, the In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Devices Regulation (IVDR) 2017/746 entered into force. In addition to current regulations, this regulation imposes a number of new requirements on the activities of pathology departments that develop and use in-house in vitro diagnostic medical devices (IH-IVDs). However, not all of these requirements have to be implemented immediately. An amending regulation of the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union will introduce the requirements for IH-IVDs in a series of stages. The current requirements, which have to be implemented from May 2022, demand conformity with the essential safety and performance requirements listed in annex I. The article presents a possible approach for implementing the valid IH-IVD regulations in the context of the current laboratory infrastructure and workflow at the Institute of Pathology at the University Hospital of Heidelberg, in order to provide some assistance to other departments. Of course, the implementation of the IH-IVD requirements will also necessitate considerable engagement from other pathology departments. However, some of the guidance documents and handouts that describe the implementation and harmonisation of the requirements for healthcare institutions mentioned in Article 5 (5) are already available and should help with the necessary coordination. An important factor for the implementation of the IVDR is also cooperation in academic networks. The conformity of university and nonuniversity institutions with the IVDR will be a further challenge in terms of personnel and time, in addition to the tasks of patient care, teaching and research and further development of methods for modern diagnostics and quality assurance and ongoing quality management.
